gallery.text.1: I based my heater on this picture.
gallery.text.2: A piece of 1/4"x.s. 4" square, steel pipe formed the base. Not being the proud owner of oxy gear, or a plasma cutter, I improvised.
gallery.text.3: Crude but effective. Two holes cut for the feed-pipe and the exhaust.
gallery.text.4: 4" dia gal pipe cut into two sections and tried for fit.
gallery.text.5: Heater core welded and ready to go. (I can weld - I never claimed I could do it well - but my grinding is a thing to behold!!)
gallery.text.7: iIn the background, the stainless-steel drum that was used for the 'afterburner' section can be seen. A flue was buried in the front of the rear section of floor, and ran teh length of the greenhouse.
gallery.text.8: Holes dug and heater elements roughly aligned so that brickwork could be planned.
gallery.text.9: Afterburner in place over exhaust stack.
gallery.text.10: Brickwork complete. Heater in place, with afterburner. Cob has been built up in layers around the stainless drum. It's a mix of excavated clay from the walk-way, straw, water and sodium silicate made from waste silica gel.
gallery.text.12: The draw was a little insufficient but the heater still burnt well enough.
gallery.text.13: Chimney. The chimney pot is constructed from the top section of my old, stainless steel, dunny-brush holder which was a nice interference fit inside of the flue, and a stainless saucepan lid. Three supporting struts are pop-rivetted with stainless rivets.
gallery.text.14: Construction of a small annexe, contiguous with the greenhouse atmosphere. The thermal mass was intentionally placed away from plants.
gallery.text.15: Thermal mass protected by the annexe.
